spellcasting.name: Spellcasting
headerEntries: The mawkeeper is a 9th-level spellcaster. Its spellcasting ability is Intelligence (spell save {@dc 14}, {@hit 6} to hit with spell attacks). It has the following wizard spells prepared:

trait.name: Amorphous
entries: The amoeboid can compress its body enough to squeeze through a 1-inch wide space.

action.name: Arc Baton
entries: {@atk mw} {@hit 3} to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. {@h}3 ({@damage 1d6}) lightning damage, and the target can't take reactions until the start of its next turn.

action.name: Phaser
entries: {@atk rw} {@hit 5} to hit, range 50/200 ft., one target. {@h}5 ({@damage 2d4}) radiant damage.

action.name: Flexible Form
entries: The amoeboid stretches, compresses, or molds its body into a static form of its choice that is no larger than 8 feet in any dimension. It can replicate something's basic shape, but not its color, texture, moving parts, or fine details. The amoeboid can't change its body's volume, nor can it move, attack, or cast spells while transformed into a static shape.

Alternatively, the amoeboid can touch a creature and assume its general form. Its size and other statistics don't change, but it gains the ability to speak and understand that creature's language, and if the target has a burrowing, climbing, or swimming speed, the amoeboid gains those with the same speed, up to a maximum of 40 feet.
The amoeboid can end a transformation on its turn (no action required). If transformed into a creature, it can only take actions that creature would be able to take.

reaction.name: Reform
entries: When the amoeboid takes bludgeoning, piercing, or slashing damage from a nonmagical source and doesn't drop to 0 hit points, it can immediately regain {@dice 1d4} hit points, up to a maximum of the amount of damage taken.

{@skill Technology|DarkMatter} Amoeboid mawkeepers maintain various positions on their maws, each overseeing or maintaining a specific function of the station. As a matter of course, mawkeepers are trained in the use of spells and are educated the intricacies of magical technology. If a threat should arise and the Sepulcher Knights falter, these mawkeepers are well-equipped to defend their stations.

monster.name: Android
languages: any three languages (usually Common, Dwarven, and Elvish)

trait.name: Black Box
entries: The android instantly can recall anything it has ever seen or heard with perfect clarity. With a successful {@dc 15} Intelligence (Technology) check, these memories can be recovered from the body of a dead android. A failed check permanently corrupts the data.

trait.name: False Identity
entries: The android is indistinguishable from an ordinary humanoid and has forged documentation to prove its identity. A {@dc 15} Intelligence (Investigation) reveals that the documents are forgeries.

trait.name: Machine Empathy
entries: The android can communicate with and understand the emotions of constructs and non- sentient machines as if it shared a language with them.

trait.name: Self-Repair
entries: The android regains 4 hit points at the start of its turn if it has at least one hit point. If the android takes lightning damage, this trait doesn't function at the start of the android's next turn.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The android makes two attacks.

action.name: Construct Surge (1/Day)
entries: The android makes four attacks, instead of two, on its turn. It can't attack on the following turn.

action.name: Repeater
entries: {@atk rw} {@hit 8} to hit, range 60/240 ft., one target. {@h}7 ({@damage 2d6}) radiant damage.

action.name: Strike
entries: {@atk mw} {@hit 8} to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. {@h}11 ({@damage 2d4 + 6}) bludgeoning damage.

Robotic shells designed to look, move, and act like humanoids, some androids begin to believe they are actually alive. The vast majority, however, act out their parts as sociable constructs, mimicking humanoid behaviors to look more convincingly alive. To most, this charade is amazingly realistic, but under the surface, androids are still nothing more than sophisticated circuitry hooked up to a humming construct core.

entries.name: Replicant Servitors
entries: Conventional automatons excel at menial tasks, and can be programmed to perform a wide variety of labors, but simply can't connect to humanoids on a social level. Androids, by contrast, were designed to fill exactly that role.

Hegemony engineers pioneered android technology in an effort to boost human expansion. An android's human- like skin conceals a powerful robotic frame underneath, capable of lifting many times its own weight. Were it not for hard-coded instructions to limit this strength, an android could easily crush its human masters. This incredible might, along with their ability to survive in a total vacuum, makes them ideal for long-term exploration and colonization missions.

entries.name: Version 13
entries: Early models of androids suffered from an extremely unnerving resemblance to humanoids, but later models made vast strides in approximating convincing humanoid appearance and behavior. By Version 10, androids were perfectly convincing in controlled circumstances. Versions 11 and 12 expanded their versatility and implemented rigorous learning systems to better copy the intelligence of living humanoids. Version 13 was a disaster.

Millions of Version 13 androids were made and shipped around the galaxy, but within the year, it was clear something was wrong with the model. Some androids malfunctioned, while others escaped their masters and vanished completely. Many Version 13 androids began to suspect they were actually alive, and learned to deactivate their fail-safes, as well as the fail-safes of other constructs. After a series of android-perpetuated murders on High Terra, things reached a fever pitch, and the entire line was to be 'recalled'—that is, destroyed and sold for scrap.

Thousands of Version 13 androids escaped destruction, and remain at large to this day, blending in perfectly with humanoids societies around the galaxy. These androids are functionally immortal, unlike newer android models, but are constantly hunted by Hegemony authorities, seeking to correct the mistake. Newer versions of androids sometimes also go rogue and escape detection for some time, but newer safety measures, along with an enforced lifespan, have kept them from becoming as prolific and singularly dangerous as the Version 13s.

entries.name: Constructed Nature
entries: An android doesn't require air, food, drink, or sleep.

trait.name: Spider Climb
entries: The arachno-horse can climb difficult surfaces, including upside down on ceilings, without needing to make an ability check.

trait.name: Trampling Charge
entries: If the arachno-horse moves at least 20 feet straight toward a target and then hits it with a bite attack on the same turn, the target must succeed on a {@dc 14} Strength saving throw or be knocked {@condition prone}. If the target is {@condition prone}, the arachno-horse can make another attack with its bite against it as a bonus action.

action.name: Bite
entries: {@atk mw} {@hit 6} to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. {@h}7 ({@damage 1d6 + 4}) piercing damage plus 3 ({@damage 1d6}) poison damage.

trait.name: Mega Creature
entries: This creature is far larger than Gargantuan size. It occupies an area 500 feet long by 500 feet wide.

trait.name: Asteroid Inhabitant
entries: The elemental moves normally in an asteroid field and takes no damage from colliding with other asteroids.

trait.name: False Appearance
entries: While the elemental remains motionless, it is indistinguishable from an ordinary asteroid.

trait.name: Magnetic Movement
entries: The elemental can sense the presence of any object within a 1,000-mile radius that is larger than 100 feet on a side and is made from metal. When moving in the direction of such an object, the elemental's flying speed increases by 2,000 feet. If the elemental strikes an object, it can immediately make one slam attack.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The elemental makes two slam attacks.

action.name: Slam
entries: {@atk mw} {@hit 10} to hit, reach 500 ft., one target. {@h}32 ({@dice 4d12 + 6}) mega bludgeoning damage.

As any pilot worth their salt knows, navigating an asteroid field is a harrowing task that requires careful planning and nerves of steel. But some smugglers have returned from jobs with tales of rogue asteroids that chase their ships, moving with murderous intent and grasping arms.

Asteroid elementals are enormous earth elementals that exist only in space, where the lack of gravity allows them to take on titanic proportions. They typically reside in asteroid fields and are comprised of elements similar to the asteroids around them, with precious metals and gemstones at their cores. This makes hunting asteroid elementals a dangerous yet rewarding task for the enterprising miner.

In order to move through the frictionless environment of deep space, asteroid elementals have developed a controlled magnetic field, which they can use to propel themselves. As a result, they are extremely sensitive to nearby metallic objects and will aggressively pursue ships that skirt too close to their territory.

entries.name: Mega Elementals
entries: Vast, wandering titans of living elemental might, mega elementals are not summoned to our plane, but are already here, formed by cosmic intersections between the Material and Elemental Planes.

entries.name: Elemental Titans
entries: On the various Elemental Planes, the elements are spontaneously imbued with life, creating formless elemental spirits. These spirits normally remain as mere wisps of elemental energy, unless summoned to the Material Plane and shaped into a physical form by magic.

However, this isn't the only way an elemental can find its way to the Material Plane. In places of catastrophic elemental energy, such as on the surface of a star or in the heart of an asteroid field, elemental bridges can spontaneously open, allowing elemental spirits to spill forth. When this happens, however, there exists no magic to give form to the new elemental spirits, which begin to conglomerate and draw power from their environment. The end result is an elemental of immense might and scale.

entries.name: Cosmic Natives
entries: Mega elementals can't be summoned or compelled into existence by magic; they are an accident of planar intersections and can normally only be found in places of great elemental energy. This means that, paradoxically, the largest and most powerful elementals aren't native to the Elemental Planes, but can be found all across the cosmos of the Material.

entries.name: Elemental Nature
entries: An elemental doesn't require air, food, drink, or sleep.

spellcasting.name: Spellcasting
headerEntries: The apologist is an 8th-level spellcaster. Its spellcasting ability is Wisdom (spell save {@dc 15}, {@hit 7} to hit with spell attacks). It has the following cleric spells prepared:

trait.name: Divine Strike
entries: Once on each of the apologist's turns when it hits a creature with a weapon attack, the apologist can cause the attack to deal an extra 4 ({@damage 1d8}) radiant damage to the target.

trait.name: Unarmored Defense
entries: While the apologist is wearing no armor and not wielding a shield, its AC equals 10 plus its Dexterity modifier + its Wisdom modifier.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The apologist makes two weapon attacks.

action.name: Avia-Ra Sunstaff (Ranged)
entries: {@atk rw} {@hit 5} to hit, range 100/400 ft., one target. {@h}7 ({@damage 2d6}) radiant damage.

action.name: Avia-Ra Sunstaff (Melee)
entries: {@atk mw} {@hit 5} to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. {@h}6 ({@damage 1d6 + 3}) bludgeoning damage, or 7 ({@damage 1d8 + 3}) bludgeoning damage if used with two hands to make a melee attack.

The avia-ra hold a dizzying array of warrior-clergymen within their Congregation warfleet, from lowly shepherds which evangelize on far-off worlds, to mighty and eminent bishops which command the armada. But the rank of apologist is apart from the rest. Only specially- appointed avia-ra of great faith can take on the mantle of apologist, from which position they answer only to the Archbishops of the Sun. Though they are tasked with spreading the word of the Sun Above, as all avia-ra clergy are, apologists are also commanded to stifle darkness and suppress heresy, even within their own ranks. Their names are spoken of in frightful whispers by those who have turned their backs on the Sun Above.

trait.name: Mega Creature
entries: This creature is far larger than Gargantuan size. It occupies an area 2,000 feet long by 2,000 feet wide.

trait.name: Hemisphere Shell
entries: The turtle's shell is a perfectly livable environment for humanoids: it has a breathable atmosphere, water, vegetation, and a gravitational field.

trait.name: Vacuum Breath
entries: The turtle doesn't require air.

trait.name: Void Jump
entries: By spending one minute performing no actions, the turtle can perform a void jump as if it were a ship with a class 3 Dark Matter engine and the astrogation spell.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The turtle makes three attacks: two with its bite and one with its slam.

action.name: Bite
entries: {@atk mw} {@hit 9} to hit, reach 1,500 ft., one target. {@h}16 ({@dice 2d10 + 5}) mega piercing damage.

action.name: Slam
entries: {@atk mw} {@hit 9} to hit, reach 1,500 ft., one target. {@h}27 ({@dice 4d10 + 5}) mega bludgeoning damage.

action.name: Asteroid Throw
entries: {@atk rw} {@hit 9} to hit, range 6,000/24,000 ft., one target. {@h}38 ({@dice 6d10 + 5}) mega bludgeoning damage.

action.name: Entrench
entries: The turtle retreats into its shell. Until the start of its next turn, its AC becomes 23.

World Turtles are the largest living things in existence—even godly manifestations don't reach their scale. As their name suggests, world turtles are so large that entire hemispheres might grow up on their shells, complete with oceans, continents, and residents that think they live on a normal planet. Moreover, these turtles are fundamentally indestructible; their skin is tough enough to weather any assault, and they are large enough to ignore any predator..

entries.name: Venerable Youth
entries: As stoic as they become when they reach maturity, baby world turtles are playful and inquisitive, but still incredibly massive. Young world turtles love searching out adventure and playing with passing ships. Sometimes, they'll even toss asteroids at them to see if they'll catch them and throw them back.

World turtles remain in youth for thousands of years, steadily growing larger and more sluggish. When at last they have grown to full size, world turtles are too massive to take notice of most living things, and drift through the 'verse, searching for a lifelong mate. All world turtles are incredibly old, perhaps immortal; none have ever been recorded to die.

entries.name: Galactic Calls
entries: Even the smallest world turtles emit a bellowing cry through the 'verse at a subsonic frequency. Amazingly, this call echoes through space itself, traveling light years and deafening those listening on comms. With careful triangulation, it's possible to follow these cries to a world turtle, in the same way that the turtles use them to find each other in the expansive Black.

trait.name: Incorporeal Movement
entries: The swarm can move through objects as if they were difficult terrain.

trait.name: Radiate
entries: Any creature that starts its turn within 5 feet of the swarm must succeed on a {@dc 11} Constitution saving throw or take 5 ({@damage 1d10}) radiant damage.

trait.name: Siphoning Presence
entries: When a bluescreen swarm occupies the same space as a piece of magical technology, such as an arcane terminal or a blaster, that isn't being worn or carried, that device ceases to function for the next minute.

trait.name: Swarm
entries: The swarm can occupy another creature's space and vice versa, and the swarm can move through any opening large enough for a Tiny bug. The swarm can't regain hit points or gain temporary hit points.

action.name: Bites
entries: {@atk mw} {@hit 6} to hit, reach 0 ft., one target. {@h}7 ({@damage 2d6}) lightning damage, or 3 ({@damage 1d6}) lightning damage if the swarm has half of its hit points of fewer. If the target is carrying a blaster, the blaster overheats.

Software bugs are half-ethereal insects that nest within arcane terminals and ship computers, causing havoc for those who use them. These pests feed off of the power sources found within such devices and multiply rapidly when they find a steady food supply. Usually, a few software bugs can be found within any large computer system, but when these bugs become numerous enough to swarm, the best solution is usually to call a debugger, a special exterminator equipped for the task.

entries.name: Technological Immune System
entries: Contrary to popular belief, a few software bugs can actually be beneficial. Software bugs safeguard data systems from harmful construct-specific diseases, such as the N-Virus, by causing small corruptions that keep viruses from taking hold. A well-placed bug can also stop a hacker dead in their tracks by creating logic flaws within their interface rig. In this way, the disruptions caused by a few software bugs are offset by the larger protections they provide.

entries.name: Bluescreen Swarms
entries: Vast numbers of software bugs are a genuine hazard, though. In the bowels of supercomputers, or among large networks of arcane terminals, where data is exchanged rapidly, computer bugs can aggregate and multiply into bluescreen swarms, which not only bring down whatever computer system they hailed from, but also pose a serious danger to creatures nearby. Bluescreen swarms can ravage an entire ship, attacking anyone that so much as carries technology or approaches a terminal. Worse still, they can fully absorb the radiant energy of blaster bolts and emit the excess power as waves of harmful radiation.

spellcasting.name: Innate Spellcasting
headerEntries: The body snatcher's innate spellcasting ability is Charisma (spell save {@dc 14}). The body snatcher can innately cast the following spells, requiring no material components:

trait.name: Sneak Attack (1/Turn)
entries: The body snatcher deals an extra 21 ({@damage 6d6}) damage when it hits a target with a weapon attack and has advantage on the attack roll, or when the target is within 5 feet of an ally of the body snatcher that isn't incapacitated and the body snatcher doesn't have disadvantage on the attack roll.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The body snatcher makes two weapon attacks or three claw attacks.

action.name: Claw (Natural Form Only)
entries: {@atk mw} {@hit 7} to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. {@h}8 ({@damage 1d8 + 4}) piercing damage.

action.name: Flay
entries: A body snatcher touches a humanoid corpse, magically hollowing it out to leave a wearable skin suit. The skin suit is protected against decay for the next 10 days.

action.name: Skinchange
entries: The body snatcher dons or doffs a skin suit. While wearing a skin suit, the body snatcher is completely indistinguishable from the person the skin originally belonged to. Additionally, the body snatcher gains the skills and the surface memories (though not the deepest secrets) of the skin's original owner.
Furthermore, the body snatcher gains 10 temporary hit points when it dons a skin suit. If all these hit points are lost, the skin suit is destroyed, and the body snatcher reverts to its natural form.

They might look like your friends, your coworkers, or even your family—a body snatcher could be anyone, an imposter staring at you through a gruesome disguise. Originating from the icy planet Capgras, the body snatchers have infiltrated every corner of the galaxy, secretly replacing people and inserting themselves into their lives.

entries.name: Wearers of Skin
entries: Standing tall atop three spiderlike legs, body snatchers do not remotely resemble a humanoid in profile; hence, it baffles the mind to consider that they might puppeteer one from within. With some subtle conjuration magic, however, a body snatcher can open a special pocket dimension within a corpse, simultaneously hollowing it out into a limp skin suit and creating enough space for their alien tripod forms to squeeze inside. Once within, the body snatcher imbues the corpse with a semblance of life, and can even recall its surface memories.

Others might notice something has changed about the victim: their demeanor has become unsettling and distant, they no longer laugh at jokes or enjoy music of any sort, and they have forgotten important events, though they will be quick to make excuses for all these things. When a body snatcher replaces someone (especially when many body snatchers do so at once), the observant become paranoid, feeling as if they are surrounded by strangers, while the unobservant wrongly dismiss such fear as delusional.

entries.name: Secret Invaders
entries: Body snatchers meet in secret to discuss their schemes for the world they have infiltrated; this is the only time they remove their disguises. The details of their plots might vary, but generally, body snatchers first work to replace everyone in a town or village, so as to isolate a staging ground for their invasion. From this launching point, the body snatchers work to plant those of their number in the highest positions of power: political leaders, generals, scientists, and celebrities. Once in total control of the world's power and influence, they can manipulate the world to their malevolent ends.

Generally, body snatchers do not want to destroy a world or replace every person in it, but they would see it trodden upon and its population enslaved, while they bask in total opulence as its rulers. However, they will also settle for inciting cataclysmic wars to reach this end conclusion.

trait.name: Insanity
entries: If a creature would use a spell or magical effect to sense the brain's emotions or read its thoughts, the creature takes 10 ({@damage 3d6}) psychic damage.

action.name: Psionic Ray
entries: {@atk rs} {@hit 4} to hit, range 60 ft., one target. {@h}21 ({@damage 6d6}) psychic damage.

action.name: Psionic Burst
entries: Each creature within 20 feet of the brain must succeed on an {@dc 13} Wisdom saving throw, taking 12 ({@damage 4d6}) psychic damage on a failed save, or half as much on a successful one.

The hovering, jar-like constructs that wrothians use to preserve their victims' brains are filled with a nutrient sludge that keeps their contents both alive and conscious. Devoid of all their senses and subjected to indescribable psychic agony, these disembodied brains quickly go insane. The resulting device, an amalgamation of biology and steel, produces a staggering amount of psionic energy, placing these unfortunate souls among the most potent and efficient power supplies in the 'verse.

entries.name: Psionic Batteries
entries: A brain in a jar operates on the simple principle that psionic energy can be coaxed out of any intelligent being with enough stimulation; of course, nothing is more stimulating than anguish. Consequently, the brain within the jar is subjected to constant electrical impulses, preventing it from resting or feeling any semblance of relief. Though Machiavellian in its cruelty, the jars are enormously powerful, both fueling wrothian ships and nourishing wrothians directly with waves of psionic power.

entries.name: Insane Psions
entries: Though the disembodied brains captive within their jars retain the memories and personalities they had in life, their endless torment renders them unlikely to act on them. In the rare circumstance that a brain is a jar is dislodged from its socket on a wrothian ship (or if that ship is damaged beyond repair), the brain can turn its psionic energy outward, relentlessly attacking anything that moves with psionic blasts. The brain can no longer identify friend or foe, or it simply doesn't care to; the boundless torment created by the jar inevitably leads the brain on a mad rampage whenever it is loosed in the world.

entries.name: Constructed Nature
entries: A brain in a jar doesn't require air, food, drink, or sleep.

entries.name: Restoring a Brain in a Jar
entries: Reversing the horrific damage caused when someone is converted into a brain in a jar is no small task. The complete lack of a body is simply too severe to be restored with a regeneration spell. Moreover, because the individual isn't fully dead, they can't be raised unless slain first and then targeted with a true resurrection spell. However, if a living brain in a jar is taken to a wrothian creation matrix, it might be possible to restore its original body without magic. Unfortunately, all methods leave lingering psychological trauma.

monster.name: Bronze Skeleton

resist.note: from nonmagical weapons that aren't adamantine

languages: understands the languages of its creator but can't speak

trait.name: Self-Destruct
entries: If the skeleton is issued a Final Command by its creator, it will self-destruct upon the task's completion or if the task is rendered impossible to complete. Any creature within 5 feet of the skeleton when it self-destructs must make a {@dc 13} Dexterity saving throw, taking 33 ({@damage 6d10}) fire damage on a failed save, or half as much on a successful one. The skeleton is destroyed completely.

trait.name: Skull Core
entries: The skeleton's construct core is embedded in its skull. If it drops to 0 hit points, but its skull isn't destroyed outright, the skeleton returns to life with 15 HP after 1 round. When the skeleton has 0 hit points, the skull is an object with AC 10, 10 HP, and immunity to poison and psychic damage.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The skeleton makes two attacks with its slam.

action.name: Slam
entries: {@atk mw} {@hit 6} to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. {@h}8 ({@damage 1d8 + 4}) bludgeoning damage.

The simplest and easiest to construct of all metallic skeletons, bronze skeletons are typically employed by the half-dozen as guards for powerful wizards or sorcerers. As opposed to ordinary shield guardians or golems, these skeletons are more disposable, and can be sent on distant journeys with little concern for lost investment. If such a skeleton is found alone, it is doubtlessly pursuing its Final Command far from its master's watchful gaze.

entries.name: Metallic Skeletons
entries: An unholy abomination of necromancy and technology, a metallic skeleton is formed by dipping the bones of the recently dead in molten metal, usually bronze, silver, or gold, fitting a construct core into the skull, and animating the whole arrangement. What results is a silent, obedient minion, with the strengths of a construct and the lifeless determination of an undead.

entries.name: Metal and Bone
entries: Metallic skeletons are a fusion of the two ideal servants animated to life by arcane magic: obedient undead and simple constructs. The result is a servant capable of repairing nearly lethal damage to itself, pursuing complex goals in service of its master, and decimating its master's enemies. With its construct core embedded in its skull, the skeleton can summon vast reserves of arcane energy to collect itself from destruction, as long its skull remains intact. Unaware adventures normally meet their end at the skeleton's deadly gaze or are crushed in its bony grasp long before discovering this crucial weakness.

entries.name: Doom Seekers
entries: The fatality of an undead form is not lost on this construct, for it hunts its goal with unending zeal. Each metallic skeleton can accept a Final Command from its master once, which it will pursue until the objective is accomplished, or until it is destroyed, whichever comes first. No matter the complexity, the task can't be disengaged by anyone, even its master. Such Final Commands are usually reserved for missions of paramount importance or last-breath proclamations of revenge. A metallic skeleton will hunt a target to the end of the 'verse, or across the planes of existence, if necessary.

It is said that the lesser powers of the Plane of Law each control a number of metallic skeletons to hunt down and dispatch those that break universal law with impunity. These constructs, called Edicts, are imbued with special powers, and are feared by lawbreakers across the multiverse.

entries.name: Constructed Nature
entries: A metallic skeleton doesn't require air, food, drink, or sleep.

action.name: Phaser
entries: {@atk rw} {@hit 2} to hit, range 50/200 ft., one target. {@h}5 ({@damage 2d4}) radiant damage.

Functionaries, politicians, and bankers are all bureaucrats, part of the growing market of middlemen which keep the 'verse's economy ticking. Uniformly, they lack the useful skills of an adventurer, and so have no means to defend themselves or survive on an undeveloped planet, but nonetheless consider themselves important and vital to their respective stations.

trait.name: Charge
entries: If the cephalo-boar moves at least 20 feet straight toward a target and then hits it with a tentacle attack on the same turn, the target takes an extra 3 ({@damage 1d6}) bludgeoning damage.

trait.name: Relentless (Recharges after a Short or Long Rest)
entries: If the cephalo-boar takes 7 damage or less and drops to 0 hit points, it is reduced to 1 hit point instead.

action.name: Tentacle
entries: {@atk mw} {@hit 3} to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. {@h}4 ({@damage 1d6 + 1}) bludgeoning damage. If the target is Medium or smaller, it is {@condition grappled} (escape {@dc 11}) and {@condition restrained} until the grapple ends.

trait.name: Close Quarters Combat
entries: Being within 5 feet of a hostile creature doesn't impose disadvantage on the captain's ranged attack rolls.

trait.name: Impulse Pack
entries: When the captain uses its flight speed, it must begin and end its movement on the ground, otherwise it falls.

trait.name: Life Support Systems
entries: The captain wears an impulse frame, which counts as a life suit.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The captain makes three ranged attacks or three melee attacks.

action.name: Antimatter Dagger
entries: {@atk mw} {@hit 6} to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. {@h}5 ({@damage 1d4 + 3}) necrotic damage.

action.name: Concussion Grenade
entries: {@atk rw} {@hit 6} to hit, range 30 ft., one target. {@h}4 ({@damage 1d8}) thunder damage and the target is {@condition deafened} until the beginning of the captain's next turn. Additionally, each creature within 10 feet of the target must make a {@dc 12} Dexterity saving throw or also take this damage and be {@condition deafened} for the same duration.

action.name: Concussion Rifle
entries: {@atk rw} {@hit 6} to hit, range 150/600 ft., one target. {@h}9 ({@damage 2d8}) thunder damage. The captain has disadvantage on attacks with this weapon if the target is within 20 feet.

action.name: Impactor Cannon
entries: {@atk rw} {@hit 6} to hit, range 30/90 ft., one target. {@h}11 ({@damage 2d10}) radiant damage, or 13 ({@damage 2d12}) radiant damage if the target is within 15 feet of the captain. The impactor then overheats, and can't be used until the end of the captain's next turn.

action.name: Repeater
entries: {@atk rw} {@hit 6} to hit, range 60/240 ft., one target. {@h}7 ({@damage 2d6}) radiant damage.

reaction.name: Heavy Frame
entries: When the captain takes bludgeoning, piercing, or slashing damage from a nonmagical source, it can halve the damage taken.

When a Coalition marine displays great promise to their superiors, they can be promoted to a squad leadership rank. These Coalition captains are given extra training and improved gear befitting their new roles. Though they are hardly ever seen without a squad of marines nearby, they are extremely dangerous opponents in their own right.

trait.name: Close Quarters Combat
entries: Being within 5 feet of a hostile creature doesn't impose disadvantage on the marine's ranged attack rolls.

trait.name: Impulse Pack
entries: When the marine uses its flight speed, it must begin and end its movement on the ground, otherwise it falls.

trait.name: Life Support Systems
entries: The marine wears an impulse frame, which counts as a life suit.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The marine makes two ranged attacks, two melee attacks, or four attacks with its standard carbine with disadvantage.

action.name: Dagger
entries: {@atk mw,rw} {@hit 5} to hit, reach 5 ft. or range 20/60 ft., one target. {@h}5 ({@damage 1d4 + 3}) piercing damage.

action.name: Concussion Grenade
entries: {@atk rw} {@hit 5} to hit, range 30 ft., one target. {@h}4 ({@damage 1d8}) thunder damage and the target is {@condition deafened} until the beginning of the marine's next turn. Additionally, each creature within 10 feet of the target must make a {@dc 12} Dexterity saving throw or also take this damage and be {@condition deafened} for the same duration.

action.name: Ion Cannon
entries: {@atk rw} {@hit 5} to hit, range 30/90 ft., one target. {@h}7 ({@damage 2d6}) radiant damage, or 9 ({@damage 2d8}) radiant damage if the target is within 15 feet.

action.name: Standard Carbine
entries: {@atk rw} {@hit 5} to hit, range 80/320 ft., one target. {@h}7 ({@damage 2d6}) radiant damage.

Coalition marines are trained in fast-paced, hit-and-run combat using their impulse frames and close quarters weaponry to strike targets hard, fast, and without warning. Their frames allow them to operate comfortably in any environment, from frigid, airless moons, to scorching desert planets.

trait.name: Magic Resistance
entries: The collector vat has advantage on saving throws against spells and other magical effects.

trait.name: Organic Scan
entries: The collector vat knows the current hit points of any creature it can see, as well as the creature's type and any diseases or conditions afflicting it. It may prioritize humanoids with lower hit points to grapple with its tentacles and store with its Store Creature ability and it may ignore creatures with incurable diseases.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The vat makes two melee weapon attacks.

action.name: Tentacle
entries: {@atk mw} {@hit 6} to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. {@h}14 ({@damage 2d10 + 3}) bludgeoning damage, and the target is {@condition grappled} (escape {@dc 15}). Until the grapple ends, the target is {@condition restrained}. The vat can grapple up to 5 creatures at once.

action.name: Syringe
entries: {@atk mw} {@hit 6} to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. {@h}8 ({@damage 2d4 + 3}) piercing damage, and the target must make a {@dc 14} Constitution saving throw or be {@condition paralyzed} for 1 minute. A creature can repeat the saving throw at the end of each of its turns, ending the effect on itself on a success.

action.name: Store Creature
entries: The vat immerses 1 Medium humanoid currently {@condition grappled} by it in the acid-filled pod on its back, and the grapple ends. While inside the vat, the creature can't breathe, is {@condition incapacitated}, has total cover against attacks and other effects outside the vat, and takes 10 ({@damage 3d6}) acid damage at the start of each of its turns. If a creature drops to 0 hit points as a result of this damage, its body is dissolved, and its brain is transferred into one of the 10 jars on the vat's side, becoming a brain in a jar. Up to 3 creatures can be inside the vat at a time.

action.name: Release Brains (1/Day)
entries: 1 ({@dice 1d4 - 1}, minimum 1) brains in jars detach from the vat, appearing in an empty space within 10 feet of it. The brains roll their own initiative and are hostile to all creatures.

When wrothian takers descend on helpless worlds, they are escorted by collector vats, merciless constructs designed to ensnare humanoids with their mechanical tentacles, digest them, and refashion their nervous systems into brains in jars. As with other wrothian technology, the process is remarkably efficient and staggeringly cruel; another grim example of wrothian ingenuity.

entries.name: Collect, Process, Repeat
entries: Collector vats receive their directives telepathically from the primarchs. If left without orders, they pursue an autonomous loop: collect a humanoid, ingest it into the digestive soup of enzymes and reagents in the vat, dissolve its skin, muscle, and bones into condensed bio-matter, and process the remaining nervous tissue into a brain in a jar. Then, repeat.

Once a target is marked for collection, the vat is relentless in pursuing it, giving up only if recalled by the primarchs at the end of a raid. The vat is cunning, as well, showing signs of real canny when loosed to hunt a target: it can follow tracks, anticipate ambushes, and spot hiding places in which its quarry might cower.

entries.name: Constructed Nature
entries: A collector vat doesn't require air, food, drink, or sleep.

trait.name: Constructed Nature
entries: You don't require air, food, drink, or sleep.

action.name: Photon Cannon
entries: {@atk rw} Your spell attack bonus to hit, range 60/240 ft., one target. {@h}9 ({@damage 2d8}) radiant damage.

trait.name: Keen Hearing and Smell
entries: The crocadog has advantage on Wisdom (Perception) checks that rely on hearing or smell.

action.name: Bite
entries: {@atk mw} {@hit 3} to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. {@h}4 ({@damage 1d6 + 1}) piercing damage. If the target is a Small or smaller creature, it must succeed on a {@dc 11} Strength saving throw or be knocked {@condition prone}.

trait.name: Follow Protocol
entries: Unless commanded to do otherwise, the drone follows its master, staying in the master's space, or as nearby as possible. The drone can move on its master's turn in order to follow in this way.

action.name: Integrated Blaster
entries: {@atk rw} {@hit 4} to hit, range 30/120 ft., one target. {@h}5 ({@damage 2d4}) radiant damage.
These small, hovering constructs assist their masters in all manner of tasks, from fetching tools to shooting at oncoming foes.

entries.name: Remote Controlled
entries: Unlike automatons and golems, drones can't be reprogrammed to perform additional tasks; instead they are purpose-built to perform one task efficiently and are linked to a drone controller, a magic item which allows the drone's master to command it with a keystroke or a thought. Though drones can reason out the facets of a command (for example, they can ascertain where to search for a particular tool), they never act without their master's say-so.

entries.name: Constructed Nature
entries: A drone doesn't require air, food, drink, or sleep.

entries.name: Repairing a Drone
entries: If a drone drops to 0 hit points, it falls to the ground and becomes disabled. You can repair it with a DC 12 Intelligence (Technology) check and one hour of work, which you can perform during a short or long rest. On a successful check, the drone's hit points are restored, and it is reactivated if it was disabled.

trait.name: Acidic Blood
entries: A creature that damages the demi- vortirrackt while within 5 feet of it takes 4 ({@damage 1d8}) acid damage.

trait.name: Magic Resistance
entries: The demi-vortirrackt has advantage on saving throws against spells and other magical effects.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The demi-vortirrackt makes three attacks with its claws.

action.name: Claws
entries: {@atk mw} {@hit 9} to hit, reach 10 ft., one target. {@h}16 ({@damage 2d10 + 5}) slashing damage, plus an additional 11 ({@damage 2d10}) slashing damage if the target has already been hit with a claw attack this turn.

action.name: Acid Glob {@recharge 5}
entries: The demi-vortirrackt lobs a glob of acid at a point it can see within 60 feet. Each creature within a 5-foot radius of that point must make a {@dc 18} Dexterity saving throw, taking 55 ({@damage 10d10}) acid damage on a failed save, or half as much damage on a successful one.

The vortirrackt are abominable predators hailing from the far edge of the multiverse. They have pale, slimy skin, resistant to weapons, a pointed head terminating in rows of razor-sharp teeth, and six sickeningly long limbs, each with one joint more than a terrestrial creature and clawlike fingers at the end. Worse yet, these creatures are no mere beasts, as their frightful intellect demonstrates.

entries.name: Out of the Dimensional Pit
entries: The multiverse is crisscrossed by a series of dimensional pits, tunnels which snake their way through the dimensions by weaving tight circuits around the Void. Few creatures will even approach them, for their radiation can penetrate even through shielded ships. However, the vortirrackt can slink through the pits with ease, and follow them wherever they open in the multiverse—each pit a new hunting ground.

Vortirrackt, telepathic as they are, do not crowd one another. If a pit opens where there is little prey, only one beast stalks through, but if prey is abundant, two or three might hunt in a small pack.

entries.name: Insidious Hunters
entries: Vortirrackt are as ferocious as they are cunning, and will happily litter their accommodations with perilous traps to ensnare intelligent prey. Let loose for twenty-four hours on a spacecraft, a vortirrackt will convert it into a veritable dungeon of deathtraps, interwoven with passageways through air vents and conduits for the monster itself.

One by one, it slaughters and devours those in its domain, all the while tormenting them with telepathy. The creature will learn the names of its prey, their specialties, and what best to say to mock them. This doesn't seem to be for any tactical advantage; the monster just finds its food all the more delicious when it gets to know it first.

entries.name: Convoluted Metamorphosis
entries: The vortirrackt have a unique biological cycle, beginning reproduction by releasing parasitic worms. These worms mature in the wombs or eggs of other creatures until they develop into larvae large enough to burst through their container. A still-growing larva then spins itself into a chrysalis, inside which it continues maturing into a demi-vortirrackt. The whole process takes only a few days.

Strangely, the demi-vortirrackt is larger and more aggressive than its elders, but possesses none of their cunning or telepathy. Within a few weeks, it sheds its brown, muscular outer skin and emerges as a smaller, pale-skinned adult vortirrackt.

trait.name: Mega Creature
entries: This creature is far larger than Gargantuan size. It occupies an area 1,500 feet long by 1,500 feet wide.

trait.name: Legendary Resistance (3/Day)
entries: If the dragon fails a saving throw, it can choose to succeed instead.

trait.name: Vacuum Breath
entries: The dragon doesn't require air.

trait.name: Void Jump
entries: By spending one minute performing no actions, the dragon can perform a void jump as if it were a ship with a class 3 Dark Matter engine and the astrogation spell.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The dragon can use its Frightful Presence. It then makes four attacks: one with its bite, two with its claws, and one with its tail.

action.name: Bite
entries: {@atk mw} {@hit 14} to hit, reach 1,500 ft., one target. {@h}23 ({@dice 3d10 + 7}) mega piercing damage plus 7 ({@dice 2d6}) mega radiant damage. If the target is a ship, one system is randomly disabled.

action.name: Claw
entries: {@atk mw} {@hit 14} to hit, reach 1,500 ft., one target. {@h}17 ({@dice 3d6 + 7}) mega slashing damage.

action.name: Tail
entries: {@atk mw} {@hit 14} to hit, reach 1,500 ft., one target. {@h}20 ({@dice 3d8 + 7}) mega bludgeoning damage.

action.name: Frightful Presence
entries: Each creature of the dragon's choice that is within 12,000 feet of the dragon and can see it must succeed on a {@dc 19} Wisdom saving throw or become {@condition frightened} for 1 minute. A creature can repeat the saving throw at the end of each of its turns, ending the effect on itself on a success. If a creature's saving throw is successful or the effect ends for it, the creature is immune to the dragon's Frightful Presence for the next 24 hours.

action.name: Void Breath
entries: The dragon exhales void radiation in a 6,000- foot cone. Each mega creature and the pilot of each ship in that area must make a {@dc 22} Dexterity saving throw, taking 90 ({@dice 20d8}) mega radiant damage on a failed save, or half as much damage on a successful one.

legendary.name: Detect
entries: The dragon makes a Wisdom (Perception) check.

legendary.name: Tail Attack
entries: The dragon makes a tail attack.

legendary.name: Wing Attack (Costs 2 Actions)
entries: The dragon beats its wings. Each mega creature and the pilot of each ship within 1,500 feet of the dragon must succeed on a {@dc 23} Dexterity saving throw or take 17 ({@dice 3d6 + 7}) mega bludgeoning damage and be pushed 1,500 feet in a straight line away from the dragon. The dragon can then fly up to half its flying speed.

Born from an egg incubated within a dying star, an eternal dragon is an avatar of destruction and greed, far more powerful and terrifying than even the most ancient terrestrial wyrms. On midnight-black wings, they soar through space, each seeking to amass a planet-sized hoard of its own.

entries.name: Immortal Wyrms
entries: Eternal dragons have existed, quite possibly, since the beginning of time. Some say that they are the true children of the dragon gods, while others say they are fallen gods themselves, banished to the Material Plane. Like all true dragons, an eternal dragon's growth knows no bounds; however, because they are immortal and immeasurably ancient, these dragons have achieved a truly immense size.

entries.name: Void Masters
entries: Eternal dragons can manipulate the Void directly, using its space-warping properties to cross vast distances in the blink of an eye. Their wings are partially comprised of voidstuff, which propels them through space with ease. Unlike other spaceborne creatures, however, eternal dragons can project this space-warping effect from their mouths as a weapon, literally exhaling Void radiation.

entries.name: Titans of Avarice
entries: As a dragon grows, so too does its lust for material wealth. The bulk of an eternal dragon's hoard is comprised not of gold, gems, or magic swords, but artifacts of immense size. Trinkets collected by an eternal dragon might include large structures like giant statues or entire temples uprooted from their original locations, small, particularly ore-rich asteroids, and even ships of various sizes. Often, an eternal dragon will hide its hoard on a small moon or a large asteroid, hollowing out enormous caverns to form its lair.

The kobolds that dwell in an eternal dragon's planetoid lair often venerate their dragon as a god. They spend tireless years organizing the dragon's mountains of treasure, growing food for its meals, and sacrificing in its name.

trait.name: Ambusher
entries: The contractor has advantage on attack rolls against any creature it has surprised.

trait.name: Sneak Attack (1/Turn)
entries: The contractor deals an extra 14 ({@damage 4d6}) damage when it hits a target with a weapon attack and has advantage on the attack roll, or when the target is within 5 feet of an ally of the contractor that isn't incapacitated and the contractor doesn't have disadvantage on the attack roll.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The contractor makes two attacks.

action.name: Antimatter Dagger
entries: {@atk mw} {@hit 5} to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. {@h}5 ({@damage 1d4 + 3}) necrotic damage.

action.name: Gnomish Phaser
entries: {@atk rw} {@hit 5} to hit, range 50/200 ft., one target. {@h}5 ({@damage 2d4}) force damage.

Firm contractors can perform a variety of duties, from infiltration and hacking, to accounting and negotiations. In a pinch, however, a contractor is a deadly combatant, capable of expertly (and discreetly) dispatching foes.

trait.name: Mega Creature
entries: This creature is far larger than Gargantuan size. It occupies an area 1,000 feet long by 1,000 feet wide.

trait.name: Fire Form
entries: The elemental can move through a space as narrow as 50 feet wide without squeezing. A creature that touches the elemental or hits it with a melee attack takes 18 ({@dice 4d8}) mega fire damage. In addition, the elemental can enter a mega hostile creature's or ship's space and stop there. The first time it does this on a turn, that creature takes 18 ({@dice 4d8}) mega fire damage.

trait.name: Illumination
entries: The elemental sheds bright light in a 10- mile radius and dim light in an additional 10 miles.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The elemental makes two touch attacks.

action.name: Touch
entries: {@atk mw} {@hit 9} to hit, reach 500 ft., one target. {@h}27 ({@dice 4d10 + 5}) mega fire damage.

Fire elementals are very much at home inside the coronas of stars, and are as abundant there as they are on their own elemental plane. Occasionally, an elemental will absorb some of the star's energy, grow to enormous size, and venture away from its home, taking on an appearance somewhat like a solar flare.

Flare elementals drift through space eternally, largely oblivious to their surroundings, uncaring as to whether their light brings warmth and life or scorching death to whatever part of the galaxy they wander through. Those that meander across busy shipping lanes present a significant danger to passing ships, particularly because they tend to seek out and follow significant sources of heat, such as a ship's engine.

entries.name: Mega Elementals
entries: Vast, wandering titans of living elemental might, mega elementals are not summoned to our plane, but are already here, formed by cosmic intersections between the Material and Elemental Planes.

entries.name: Elemental Titans
entries: On the various Elemental Planes, the elements are spontaneously imbued with life, creating formless elemental spirits. These spirits normally remain as mere wisps of elemental energy, unless summoned to the Material Plane and shaped into a physical form by magic.

However, this isn't the only way an elemental can find its way to the Material Plane. In places of catastrophic elemental energy, such as on the surface of a star or in the heart of an asteroid field, elemental bridges can spontaneously open, allowing elemental spirits to spill forth. When this happens, however, there exists no magic to give form to the new elemental spirits, which begin to conglomerate and draw power from their environment. The end result is an elemental of immense might and scale.

entries.name: Cosmic Natives
entries: Mega elementals can't be summoned or compelled into existence by magic; they are an accident of planar intersections and can normally only be found in places of great elemental energy. This means that, paradoxically, the largest and most powerful elementals aren't native to the Elemental Planes, but can be found all across the cosmos of the Material.

entries.name: Elemental Nature
entries: An elemental doesn't require air, food, drink, or sleep.

spellcasting.name: Innate Spellcasting
headerEntries: The husk's innate spellcasting ability is Charisma (spell save {@dc 14}). The husk can innately cast the following spells, requiring no material components:

trait.name: Blood Frenzy
entries: The husk has advantage on melee attack rolls against any creature that doesn't have all its hit points.

trait.name: Magic Resistance
entries: The husk has advantage on saving throws against spells and other magical effects.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The husk makes two slam attacks.

action.name: Slam
entries: {@atk mw} {@hit 6} to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. {@h}11 ({@damage 2d8 + 2}) bludgeoning damage and the target must make a {@dc 14} Constitution saving throw.

On a failed save, the air is drawn out from the target's lungs. Until the end of the target's next turn, it has disadvantage on attack rolls, ability checks, and saving throws. This effect is not impeded by magic such as zone of atmosphere or technology such as life suits.

Few can personally attest to the ravages of being exposed to the naked vacuum of space. With rare exceptions, the vacuum is brutally lethal, stripping the body of all gases, freezing it whole, and boiling its blood in seconds. Sometimes, however, the Black is ill-content with merely stripping everything from the body; it might also divorce the soul from it, leaving behind a shriveled, undead husk, restlessly prowling to satiate its bottomless hunger and thirst.

A husk's gray skin is cracked in deep fissures and its body is impossibly gaunt, looking appropriately like it has been deprived of essential air and heat for years. As manifestations of the 'verse's bitter severity, husks represent the fate of countless spacers who have been stranded in the Black without hope of rescue.

entries.name: Grim Remnants
entries: As storied spacer Zan, the Terran, once said, 'Never search an abandoned ship without a blaster. 'Abandoned' doesn't always mean 'empty'.'

In other words, old crewmen have a tendency to linger on their derelict ships as husks, still desperately clutching at their life suits and {@item life pods|darkmatter} for air. The last moments of these spacers are hideously stretched out to an eternity of undead suffering, terminated only when they are discovered or destroyed by salvagers, or their ship plummets into a planet or star.

entries.name: Out the Airlock
entries: Spacer wisdom attests that the dead should be pushed out the airlock without exception or delay. New spacers might attribute this course of action to pragmatic disease prevention, but seasoned explorers know it's because no ship is ever truly safe from husks. In certain regions of space, bodies can unnaturally dehydrate, shriveling to the gaunt, lifeless outline of husks, without ever meeting the vacuum directly. It is a complete mystery how such husks animate, but seasoned spacers know better than to search for the cause—the answer might prove to be far worse than the question itself.

entries.name: Undead Nature
entries: A husk doesn't require air, food, drink, or sleep.

trait.name: AI Companion
entries: If the gadgeteer rolls less than 16 with any of its listed skills, it treats the result as a 16 instead.

trait.name: Overcharge
entries: A blaster deals three extra dice of its damage and its Intelligence modifier when the gadgeteer hits with it (included in the attack).

action.name: Wrenchinator
entries: {@atk mw} {@hit 7} to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. {@h}10 ({@damage 1d12 + 4}) bludgeoning damage.

action.name: Concussion Rifle
entries: {@atk rw} {@hit 6} to hit, range 150/600 ft., one target. {@h}26 ({@damage 5d8 + 4}) thunder damage. The gadgeteer has disadvantage on attacks with this weapon if the target is within 20 feet.

action.name: Repeater
entries: {@atk rw} {@hit 6} to hit, range 60/240 ft., one target. {@h}21 ({@damage 5d6 + 4}) radiant damage.

action.name: Omni-Grenade
entries: The gadgeteer throws a grenade at a point it can see within 30 feet. Each creature within a 5-foot radius of that point must make a {@dc 14} Dexterity saving throw or take 22 ({@damage 5d8}) fire damage, or half as much on a successful save.

action.name: Gadget
entries: The gadgeteer uses one of the following gadgets:

items.name: Blink Button
entries: The gadgeteer teleports up to 15 feet.

items.name: Cloaking Device {@recharge 4}
entries: The gadgeteer becomes {@condition invisible} until the end of its next turn, as per the spell {@spell invisibility}.

items.name: Overheat Beacon {@recharge 5}
entries: Each blaster within 60 feet of the gadgeteer (excluding those carried by the gadgeteer) overheat until the end of the gadgeteer's next turn.

items.name: Shrink Ray
entries: One creature within 30 feet is targeted by the Reduce effect of the enlarge/reduce spell (save {@dc 14})

Gifted inventors and engineers, gadgeteers are always pushing the boundaries of what can be accomplished with magical technology. They are almost always festooned with hardware components, useful gadgets, and tricked- out blasters, as well as accompanied by an AI, or some other mechanical companion, which assists them in every endeavor.

trait.name: Mega Creature
entries: This creature is far larger than Gargantuan size. It occupies an area 4,000 feet long by 4,000 feet wide.

trait.name: Alien Anatomy
entries: Any critical hit the gigapod suffers is treated as a regular hit instead.

trait.name: Magic Resistance
entries: The gigapod has advantage on saving throws against spells and other magical effects.

trait.name: Vacuum Breath
entries: The gigapod doesn't require air.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The gigapod makes four tentacle attacks or three tentacle attacks and one slam attack.

action.name: Slam
entries: {@atk mw} {@hit 11} to hit, reach 500 ft., one target. {@h}33 ({@dice 5d10 + 6}) mega bludgeoning damage.

action.name: Tentacle
entries: {@atk mw} {@hit 11} to hit, reach 2,000 ft., one target. {@h}22 ({@dice 3d10 + 6}) mega bludgeoning damage.

reaction.name: Protective Instinct
entries: When a creature that the gigapod can see within 3,000 feet deals damage to a creature that the gigapod is friendly towards, the gigapod can move up to 3,000 feet towards the attacker and make one tentacle attack against it.

When old spacers gather at hearthstations to tell stories of the strange things they've seen in the 'verse (as they often do), someone invariably mentions the gigapods, often misnamed 'the moon eaters.' These sprawling, vast amalgamations of tentacles and strange organs are as alien as anything can be, for they lack anything even resembling a singular body, head, or central point. Indeed, their bizarre lifecycle and incomprehensible anatomy points to an origin outside the fringes of civilized space.

entries.name: Planetary Gestation
entries: Gigapods have a number of special tentacles, each equipped with a solitary gigaseed that does not regrow once detached. Descending on small planetoids, moons, and asteroids large enough to harbor a warm core, the gigapods bore an enormous seed-bearing tentacle deep into the hot mantle, where the seed is deposited. In the heat, the seed swells to an incredible size and, through completely unknown mechanisms, metamorphoses into a young gigapod. Once it is large enough, the gigapod hatches from the planetoid, smashing it like an egg.

Gigaseeds are curiously small—less than two feet in diameter—but are best regarded as weapons comparable to arcane warheads. A gigaseed can seemingly last indefinitely before being hatched within a planetoid's core to wreak utter devastation when it emerges. In the wrong hands, a single seed delivered by a large drill or sophisticated teleportation spell can lay waste to an entire world.

entries.name: Adopters
entries: When a gigapod has used all of its seeds, its demeanor transforms completely. Young gigapods wander the 'verse in search of new planetoids, violently attacking interlopers that draw too near, while older gigapods are more curious of ships and creatures they encounter and have even been known to 'adopt' smaller creatures, which they perhaps even view as surrogate children. When a gigapod adopts a creature, it is violently protective, striking out against others that endanger or even crowd too close to their surrogate child.

entries.name: Across the Black Ocean
entries: Nothing discovered in the 'verse begins to compare with the biology of the gigapod, leaving only a few possible origins. Perhaps a lone gigaseed was flung at incredible speeds by a climactic event in a far-off galaxy, propelling across the Black Ocean, the expanse of empty space between galaxies. Most scholars believe this to be the most likely scenario, but the other possibilities are just as likely and far more terrifying.

It is possible that gigapods are the result of bizarre magical tampering with the fabric of the universe. Perhaps, the gigapods originate from a foreign dimension, not simply a far-off galaxy. If that were the case, far more of these creatures might reside far closer than anyone could have anticipated; merely a spell away, in a dimension where the laws of physics differ from our own. To open a portal to this dimension, if it indeed exists, could be devastating for all life in the 'verse.

entries.name: Emergence Event
entries: Planting and hatching a gigaseed should be a pivotal moment in a campaign. Once a seed is embedded within a planet's hot core, it lays dormant for precisely 26 years as it grows and develops into a young gigapod. This intervening period is marked by increasingly turbulent volcanic activity, earthquakes, and cataclysmic storms, giving adventurers time to plan out how best to save the world and its inhabitants, whether by evacuation, magical intervention, or a daring expedition to the planet's core. Moreover, it presents a rigidly ticking clock for the campaign's action. When the time is at last up, the gigapod explodes forth from the planet's core, ripping a hole hundreds of miles across on the planet's surface and devastating all life on it.

trait.name: Mega Creature
entries: This creature is far larger than Gargantuan size. It occupies an area 1,500 feet long by 1,500 feet wide.

trait.name: Legendary Resistance (3/Day)
entries: If the aboleth fails a saving throw, it can choose to succeed instead.

trait.name: Transformative Radiation
entries: The aboleth constantly emits strange radiation. A creature that starts its turn within 500 feet of the aboleth and isn't shielded against radiation (including creatures aboard ships) must make a {@dc 19} Constitution saving throw or be diseased for {@dice 1d4} hours. The diseased creature can only breathe in a vacuum and must hold its breath in any kind of atmosphere.

trait.name: Vacuum Breath
entries: The aboleth doesn't require air.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The aboleth makes one tail attack and four tentacle attacks. It can substitute any number of tentacle attacks with God's Touches.

action.name: Tail
entries: {@atk mw} {@hit 13} to hit, reach 1,000 ft., one target. {@h}17 ({@dice 3d6 + 7}) mega bludgeoning damage.

action.name: Tentacle
entries: {@atk mw} {@hit 13} to hit, reach 1,000 ft., one target. {@h}14 ({@dice 2d6 + 7}) mega bludgeoning damage.

action.name: Enslave {@recharge 3}
entries: The aboleth touches one creature that can see it within 3,000 feet of it. The target must succeed on a {@dc 19} Wisdom saving throw or be magically {@condition charmed} by the aboleth until the aboleth dies or until it is on a different plane of existence from the target. The {@condition charmed} target is under the aboleth's control and can't take reactions, and the aboleth and the target can communicate telepathically with each other over any distance.

Whenever the {@condition charmed} target takes damage, the target can repeat the saving throw. On a success, the effect ends. No more than once every 24 hours, the target can also repeat the saving throw when it is at least 1,000 miles away from the aboleth.

action.name: God's Touch
entries: The aboleth reaches out and touches a creature within its reach, which must succeed on a {@dc 19} Constitution saving throw or become diseased. The disease has no effect for 1 minute and can be removed by any magic that cures disease. After 1 minute, the diseased creature's skin becomes translucent and sparkly, the creature can't regain hit points unless it is in a vacuum, and the disease can be removed only by heal or another disease-curing spell of 6th level or higher. Each minute the creature is not in a vacuum, it takes 6 ({@damage 1d12}) acid damage.

action.name: Raise (3/Day)
entries: The aboleth targets the corpse of one dead creature it can see within 3,000 feet of it. As long as its soul is at liberty to rejoin the body, the target returns to life with 1 hit point, and is immediately subjected to the aboleth's Enslave ability (even if it currently needs to recharge), with disadvantage on the saving throw.

legendary.name: Detect
entries: The aboleth makes a Wisdom (Perception) check.

legendary.name: Tail Attack
entries: The aboleth makes a tail attack.

legendary.name: Psychic Drain (Costs 2 Actions)
entries: One creature {@condition charmed} by the aboleth takes 28 ({@damage 8d6}) psychic damage, and the aboleth regains mega hit points equal to the damage the creature takes.

Since ancient times, aboleths have dreamed of overthrowing the gods and ruling the universe, just as they did before the gods came to be. Unfortunately for all other living things, some of the oldest and mightiest of these foul creatures are close to succeeding.

entries.name: Ascended Aberrations
entries: Aboleths plot their ascents to power over spans of millennia, arranging an intricate web of schemes, gambits, strategic moves, and dark rituals. In a 'verse full of zealous adventurers and vigilant deities, such care is necessary, and it serves to ensure that a slow trickle of aboleths are able to slip their watery bonds and metamorphose into great, interstellar wanderers.

Having achieved their ultimate forms, however, 'god- aboleths' are generally dissatisfied with their newfound might. Their titanic bodies may possess horrific strength, but they are still weaker than even the lowest of demigods, and therefore unable to achieve what they see as their rightful place in the divine pantheon. For this reason, god-aboleths travel through the stars, looking for forgotten secrets and hidden mysteries, hoping that one day they will find a way to become gods in more than just name.

entries.name: Enslavers of Empires
entries: God-aboleths aren't shy about enslaving lesser creatures. Most acquire large flocks of devotees, each one more mindless and enthralled than the last. Imagining that these magically-enslaved peons are true disciples, god-aboleths use them to work their will on the universe, issuing pseudo-divine commandments and meting out blessings or retribution as they see fit. The aboleth's slaves follow their god, setting up temples on airless moons and hijacking crippled spacecraft as they go.

monster.name: Gold Skeleton

resist.note: from nonmagical weapons that aren't adamantine

languages: understands the languages of its creator but can't speak

trait.name: Magic Resistance
entries: The skeleton has advantage on saving throws against spells and other magical effects.

trait.name: Self-Destruct
entries: If the skeleton is issued a Final Command by its creator, it will self-destruct upon the task's completion or if the task is rendered impossible to complete. Any creature within 5 feet of the skeleton when it self-destructs must make a {@dc 18} Dexterity saving throw, taking 55 ({@damage 10d10}) fire damage on a failed save, or half as much on a successful one. The skeleton is destroyed completely.

trait.name: Skull Core
entries: The skeleton's construct core is embedded in its skull. If it drops to 0 hit points, but its skull isn't destroyed outright, the skeleton returns to life with 45 hit points after 1 round. When the skeleton has 0 hit points, the skull is an object which has AC 13, 20 hit points, and immunity to poison and psychic damage.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The skeleton makes three attacks with its slam or two attacks with its slam and one with its eye beams.

action.name: Eye Beams
entries: The skeleton fires scorching beams from its eyes in a line that is 30 feet long and 5 feet wide. Each creature in that line must make a {@dc 18} Dexterity saving throw, taking 45 ({@damage 6d10 + 12}) radiant damage on a failed save, or half as much on a successful one.

action.name: Slam
entries: {@atk mw} {@hit 11} to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. {@h}15 ({@damage 2d8 + 6}) bludgeoning damage.

Gifted with immense power by their masters, these gilded skeletons are true grave lords, as costly as they are mighty. Destroying one completely is a feat in and of itself, for while gold isn't the most rigid of metals, it provides an ample framework for insulating necromancy magic, which reinforces and empowers the construct core within the skeleton's skull.

entries.name: Metallic Skeletons
entries: An unholy abomination of necromancy and technology, a metallic skeleton is formed by dipping the bones of the recently dead in molten metal, usually bronze, silver, or gold, fitting a construct core into the skull, and animating the whole arrangement. What results is a silent, obedient minion, with the strengths of a construct and the lifeless determination of an undead.

entries.name: Metal and Bone
entries: Metallic skeletons are a fusion of the two ideal servants animated to life by arcane magic: obedient undead and simple constructs. The result is a servant capable of repairing nearly lethal damage to itself, pursuing complex goals in service of its master, and decimating its master's enemies. With its construct core embedded in its skull, the skeleton can summon vast reserves of arcane energy to collect itself from destruction, as long its skull remains intact. Unaware adventures normally meet their end at the skeleton's deadly gaze or are crushed in its bony grasp long before discovering this crucial weakness.

entries.name: Doom Seekers
entries: The fatality of an undead form is not lost on this construct, for it hunts its goal with unending zeal. Each metallic skeleton can accept a Final Command from its master once, which it will pursue until the objective is accomplished, or until it is destroyed, whichever comes first. No matter the complexity, the task can't be disengaged by anyone, even its master. Such Final Commands are usually reserved for missions of paramount importance or last-breath proclamations of revenge. A metallic skeleton will hunt a target to the end of the 'verse, or across the planes of existence, if necessary.

It is said that the lesser powers of the Plane of Law each control a number of metallic skeletons to hunt down and dispatch those that break universal law with impunity. These constructs, called Edicts, are imbued with special powers, and are feared by lawbreakers across the multiverse.

entries.name: Constructed Nature
entries: A metallic skeleton doesn't require air, food, drink, or sleep.

spellcasting.name: Innate Spellcasting (Psionics)
headerEntries: The psi-worm gestalt's spellcasting ability is Wisdom (spell save {@dc 18}). It can innately cast the following spells, requiring no material components:

trait.name: Alien Movement
entries: The gestalt can writhe more quickly over rough ground, moving 2 feet in difficult terrain for every 1 foot of movement it expends.

trait.name: Psychic Defense
entries: The gestalt's AC equals 10 + its Dexterity modifier + its Wisdom modifier.

trait.name: Swarm
entries: The gestalt can occupy another creature's space and vice versa, and the gestalt can move through any opening large enough for a Tiny worm. The gestalt can't regain hit points or gain temporary hit points.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The gestalt makes a slam attack and then makes up to two attacks with its bites against different targets.

action.name: Bites
entries: {@atk mw} {@hit 7} to hit, reach 0 ft., one creature in the gestalt's space. {@h}25 ({@damage 10d4}) piercing damage or 12 ({@dice 5d4}) if the gestalt has half of its hit points or fewer. A creature that has been hit by this attack must make a {@dc 15} Constitution saving throw or be infested with 4 psi-worms (see psi-worm stats). A humanoid slain by this attack rises 1 round later as a worm walker.

action.name: Slam
entries: {@atk mw} {@hit 7} to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. {@h}14 ({@damage 2d10 + 3}) bludgeoning damage.

When humanoids arrived on Kiron, the first of many discovered worlds inhabited with psi-worms, they thought they had found paradise: a bountiful and fertile land, rich in natural magic, and apparently unmarred by the idiosyncratic designs of any intelligent native life. The planet was colonized aggressively. For a time, the colonies flourished, but peace was not to last.

entries.name: Psionic Wardens
entries: As the colonies expanded, they began to encounter large swarms of parasitic worms. To the colonists' horror, when enough worms amassed at once, they formed a gestalt consciousness that was not only intelligent and hostile, but also capable of mounting psionic attacks that would leave humanoids paralyzed in fear—easy prey for the worms.

Gradually, a pattern emerged, whereby the psi-worms would leave small pockets of humanoids unmolested, but ferociously attack larger settlements, especially those that consumed the planet's resources at an unsustainable rate. It became clear that the worms were acting as a defense mechanism for the planet itself, and that some kind of psionic super-consciousness was coordinating them, calling up swarms of worms whenever the environment was threatened.

entries.name: Fearless Drones
entries: Psi-worms obey the commands of the collective instinctively and automatically. They have no capacity for independent thought and only behave with intelligence when formed into a gestalt. When psi-worms attack, they can't be negotiated with, driven off, starved out, or forced to surrender—psi-worms will fight until one side is destroyed.

entries.name: Brain Burrowers
entries: Humanoids that fall to psi-worms face a far more gruesome fate than death: as a psi-worm larvae burrows into its skull, it roots itself in the brain- stem and seizes control of the creature, rendering them mindless. Until the threat has passed, these new worm walkers fight alongside the psi-worm gestalts to drive off invaders. However, when they are no longer needed, they are merely abandoned and left to fertilize the nearest patch of xenobloom.

trait.name: Comm Link
entries: The trooper is in constant communication and coordination with its allies within 2 miles of it. It can't be surprised by any creature or object that any of its allies within range are aware of.

trait.name: Life Suit
entries: The trooper's armor contains an integrated life suit.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The trooper makes two attacks with its standard carbine, two attacks with its arc baton, or four attacks with its standard carbine with disadvantage.

action.name: Arc Baton
entries: {@atk mw} {@hit 4} to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. {@h}5 ({@damage 1d6 + 2}) lightning damage. If the target is a creature, it can't take reactions until the start of its next turn.

action.name: Concussion Grenade
entries: {@atk rw} {@hit 4} to hit, range 30 ft., one target. {@h}4 ({@damage 1d8}) thunder damage and the target is {@condition deafened} until the beginning of the trooper's next turn. Additionally, each creature within 10 feet of the target must make a {@dc 12} Dexterity saving throw or also take this damage and be {@condition deafened} for the same duration.

action.name: Standard Carbine
entries: {@atk rw} {@hit 5} to hit, range 80/320 ft., one target. {@h}7 ({@damage 2d6}) radiant damage.

Hegemony troopers are the backbone of the Hegemony of Man's military. Outfitted with high-quality tactical gear and trained in all manner of combat, these troopers fill a variety of roles, from foot soldiers on the battlefield, to military police on human worlds.

trait.name: Blood Frenzy
entries: The husk has advantage on melee attack rolls against any creature that doesn't have all its hit points.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The husk makes two slam attacks.

action.name: Slam
entries: {@atk mw} {@hit 4} to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. {@h}6 ({@damage 1d8 + 2}) bludgeoning damage and the target must make a {@dc 12} Constitution saving throw.

On a failed save, the air is drawn out from the target's lungs. Until the end of the target's next turn, it has disadvantage on attack rolls, ability checks, and saving throws. This effect is not impeded by magic such as zone of atmosphere or technology such as life suits.

Few can personally attest to the ravages of being exposed to the naked vacuum of space. With rare exceptions, the vacuum is brutally lethal, stripping the body of all gases, freezing it whole, and boiling its blood in seconds. Sometimes, however, the Black is ill-content with merely stripping everything from the body; it might also divorce the soul from it, leaving behind a shriveled, undead husk, restlessly prowling to satiate its bottomless hunger and thirst.

A husk's gray skin is cracked in deep fissures and its body is impossibly gaunt, looking appropriately like it has been deprived of essential air and heat for years. As manifestations of the 'verse's bitter severity, husks represent the fate of countless spacers who have been stranded in the Black without hope of rescue.

entries.name: Grim Remnants
entries: As storied spacer Zan, the Terran, once said, 'Never search an abandoned ship without a blaster. 'Abandoned' doesn't always mean 'empty'.'

In other words, old crewmen have a tendency to linger on their derelict ships as husks, still desperately clutching at their life suits and {@item life pods|darkmatter} for air. The last moments of these spacers are hideously stretched out to an eternity of undead suffering, terminated only when they are discovered or destroyed by salvagers, or their ship plummets into a planet or star.

entries.name: Out the Airlock
entries: Spacer wisdom attests that the dead should be pushed out the airlock without exception or delay. New spacers might attribute this course of action to pragmatic disease prevention, but seasoned explorers know it's because no ship is ever truly safe from husks. In certain regions of space, bodies can unnaturally dehydrate, shriveling to the gaunt, lifeless outline of husks, without ever meeting the vacuum directly. It is a complete mystery how such husks animate, but seasoned spacers know better than to search for the cause—the answer might prove to be far worse than the question itself.

entries.name: Undead Nature
entries: A husk doesn't require air, food, drink, or sleep.

monster.name: Indoctrinated
languages: one language it knew before indoctrination

trait.name: Cybernetic Enhancements
entries: The indoctrinated is immune to nonmagical diseases, and it doesn't need to eat, drink, or breathe. Additionally, it can't be disarmed of its weapons.

action.name: Arm Cannon
entries: {@atk mw,rw} {@hit 4} to hit, reach 5 ft. or range 60/240 ft., one creature. {@h}9 ({@damage 2d8}) radiant damage.

Little more than a humanoid shell of their former self, an indoctrinated was converted by mechademons into a grim reflection of their malevolence. Unleashed indoctrinated employ downright suicidal tactics—detonating explosives and charging into fire whenever possible—for if they could still speak, they would beg for death.

entries.name: Mechademons
entries: Demons—fiends which hail from the foulest abyss—reflect the darkest aspects of mankind. Their evil is boundless and chaotic, yet disquietingly familiar, as if it were born from all the failings of the Material Plane. There seems to be some truth to this notion, for as humanoids clawed their way to the stars and surrounded themselves with great machines and forests of steel, demons changed as well, incorporating savage mechanistic enhancements into their already corrupt forms. These mechademons are doom incarnate, the fate awaiting the soul of every evil individual who dies among the stars.

entries.name: Evil Enhanced
entries: Evil Enhanced. Every mechademon is unique, having surgically inserted brutalistic machines into their bodies to upgrade themselves in various ways. Usually, this makes them tougher, more agile, and purpose-built for cruelty. For example, a razor-sharp forearm blade won't be more useful in combat than a dagger, but it's lovely for torture.

entries.name: Indoctrination
